title: Vision Acuity Examinations and Charts
narrationScript: Visual testing certification rests on a simple premise. The examiner's eyes are the measuring instrument, so they have to be verified like any other instrument. Let's go through the vision examinations that qualify a visual testing inspector, the charts used to run them, and the color and gray shade requirements that go with them. Personnel qualification standards and employer written practices require documented vision examinations before an inspector is certified, and at set intervals afterward. Two traits get tested. Near vision acuity, and color or gray shade perception. Near vision is emphasized because almost all visual testing is close range reading of fine detail, held at arm's length or nearer. A crack, a pore, a gauge scale, a written report. Distance vision matters far less to the task, so the standards concentrate on the near test and require it often. The workhorse is the near vision acuity test. The examiner reads a standardized chart of letters, numbers, or text in graduated sizes at a specified close distance, and the smallest line read correctly defines the acuity. Several chart systems are common. Jaeger charts present paragraphs of text in numbered sizes, from large down to fine, and a typical visual testing requirement is the ability to read Jaeger two, or smaller, at the distance printed on the card, often around twelve inches, in at least one eye, corrected or uncorrected. Times Roman, or N notation, charts use point sizes for the same purpose. And Snellen equivalent near cards express near acuity in the familiar twenty twenty form for a reduced reading distance. Whatever chart is specified, the rules are the same. Read at the stated distance, under adequate lighting, with the correction normally worn, in the eyes the standard requires. The test is repeated on a schedule, commonly at least annually, and any time vision is suspected to have changed. Now, even though visual testing leans on near vision, you should understand the distance acuity system, because its notation is everywhere. Distance acuity is measured with a wall chart of letters in decreasing sizes read at a fixed range, the Snellen chart being the classic example. The result is written as a ratio such as twenty twenty. The top number is the test distance in feet, and the bottom number is the distance at which a person with normal vision could read that same line. So twenty forty means you read at twenty feet what a normal eye reads at forty, which is worse than standard. Physically, twenty twenty corresponds to resolving detail about one arc minute across. Keep in mind that real world acuity is always lower than the chart number, because contrast, lighting, motion, and surface condition all degrade what you can actually resolve on a part. Beyond acuity, many examinations demand that the inspector can tell colors and shades apart. Color vision deficiency, most commonly red green, affects a notable share of men, and often goes unnoticed by the person who has it, so it is screened directly. The classic tool is the pseudoisochromatic plate test. Numbers or paths formed from colored dots embedded in a field of differently colored dots, readable by normal color vision and confused by a deficient observer. Color matters in real work. Temper colors on stainless steel, the color of corrosion products, a dye penetrant indication against its developer, and color coded acceptance marking. Gray shade differentiation is the related ability to separate subtle differences in brightness, which underpins reading low contrast features and is especially important for remote and recorded visual testing on a monitor. Finally, the examination only counts if it is done and recorded properly. It is administered by a qualified person or facility named in the written practice, and the result becomes part of the certification record, traceable to who, by whom, which chart, what distance, the result, and the date. Three rules to carry. Corrected vision is acceptable, and that correction must be worn on the job. Requalification is periodic, commonly at least annually for near acuity. And failure means restriction, not necessarily the end, since an examiner may retest after correction or be limited to examinations that do not need the trait they lack. The thread through all of it is the written practice. Read it, follow it, and keep your vision documentation current.

sections.heading: Why Vision Exams Are Required
sections.body:
VT certification rests on a simple premise: the examiner's eyes are the measuring instrument, so they have to be verified like any other instrument. Personnel qualification standards and employer written practices therefore require documented vision examinations before an inspector is certified and at set intervals afterward. The two traits that get tested are **near-vision acuity** and **color (or gray-shade) perception**, because those are exactly the abilities the work demands.

Near vision is emphasized because almost all VT is close-range reading of fine detail - a crack, a pore, a gauge scale, a written report - held at arm's length or nearer. Distance acuity matters far less to the task, so the standards concentrate on the near test and require it frequently. Color perception is tested because many examinations rely on distinguishing colors or shades: temper colors on steel, corrosion products, dye-penetrant indications, coating defects, and color-coded acceptance.

The exams are not a one-time hurdle. Vision drifts with age and health, so requalification on a defined schedule catches that drift before it costs a missed defect. Corrected vision is acceptable - the requirement is meeting the standard, with glasses or contacts if needed - and the examination simply confirms each examiner still measures up.

sections.heading: Near-Vision Acuity Tests
sections.body:
The workhorse of VT vision testing is the **near-vision acuity** test. The examiner reads a standardized chart of letters, numbers, or text in graduated sizes at a specified close distance, and the smallest line read correctly defines the acuity. Several chart systems are in common use:

- **Jaeger charts** present paragraphs of text in numbered type sizes, from large down to fine. Results are reported as a J-number; a typical VT requirement is the ability to read **Jaeger 2 (J-2)** or smaller at the distance printed on the card, commonly around **12 inches (about 300 millimetres)**, in at least one eye, corrected or uncorrected.
- **Times Roman / N-notation** charts use point sizes (for example N4, N5) for the same purpose.
- **Snellen-equivalent near cards** express near acuity in the familiar 20/20 form for a reduced reading distance.

Whichever chart is specified, the rules that matter are the same: read at the **stated distance**, under adequate lighting, with the correction normally worn, in the eye(s) the standard requires. The test is repeated on a defined schedule - commonly at least **annually** under typical written practices - and any time vision is suspected to have changed. The exact chart, distance, passing line, and frequency are set by the employer's written practice in line with the governing qualification standard, so always read the procedure rather than assuming a number.

sections.caption: Near-vision and distance acuity charts: a graduated near-reading card read at a fixed close distance versus a Snellen distance chart.
Typical VT vision examination requirements (verify against the governing written practice).
rows: Resolving fine detail at reading distance
Jaeger J-2 (or equivalent) at ~12 in / 300 mm, one eye, corrected OK
Resolving detail at range (secondary for VT)
Pass a recognized color test; demonstrate shade differentiation

sections.heading: Distance Acuity and the 20/20 Notation
sections.body:
Although VT leans on near vision, you should understand the distance-acuity system because its notation appears everywhere. Distance acuity is measured with a wall chart of letters in decreasing sizes read at a fixed range, the **Snellen** chart being the classic example. The result is written as a ratio such as **20/20**: the top number is the test distance in feet, and the bottom number is the distance at which a person with 'normal' vision could read that same line. So 20/40 means you read at twenty feet what a normal eye reads at forty - worse than standard - while 20/15 is sharper than standard.

Physically, 20/20 corresponds to resolving detail about **one arc-minute** across - roughly the finest spacing the eye can just separate at that distance. Real-world acuity is always lower than the chart number because contrast, lighting, motion, and surface condition all degrade what you can actually resolve on a part. That is the bridge back to VT: passing 20/20 on a clean, high-contrast chart in a bright room is the ceiling, not the performance you will get on a dirty weld in poor light.

sections.title: What the standard actually checks
sections.body:
Two things, on a schedule:
- **Near acuity** - read Jaeger J-2 (or equivalent) at the card's stated distance (commonly ~12 in / 300 mm), corrected or uncorrected, typically **at least annually**.
- **Color / gray-shade perception** - demonstrate the ability to distinguish the colors and shades the work requires, usually **on entry** and re-evaluated per the written practice.

Corrected vision is fine; exact charts, distances, and intervals come from the employer's written practice and the governing qualification standard.

sections.heading: Color and Gray-Shade Differentiation
sections.body:
Beyond acuity, many examinations demand that the inspector can tell colors and shades apart. **Color-vision deficiency** - most commonly red-green - affects a notable share of men and often goes unnoticed by the person who has it, so it is screened directly. The classic tool is the **pseudoisochromatic plate** test: numbers or paths formed from colored dots embedded in a field of differently colored dots, readable by normal color vision and invisible or confused to a deficient observer. Other methods include arranging colored chips in order of hue or using a calibrated anomaloscope for finer grading.

Color discrimination matters in real VT: temper and heat-tint colors that indicate over-temperature on stainless steel, the rust-red versus black of different corrosion products, the color of a dye-penetrant indication against its developer, coating and paint defects, and color-coded acceptance marking. **Gray-shade (contrast) differentiation** is the related ability to separate subtle differences in brightness - distinguishing a faint indication from background, or steps on a gray scale. It underpins reading low-contrast features and is especially important for remote and recorded VT, where a monitor reproduces the scene in limited shades.

Where color or shade discrimination is essential to a method, the qualification standard requires the examiner to demonstrate it, and an examiner who cannot may be restricted from examinations that depend on it.

sections.caption: Color and gray-shade differentiation tests: a pseudoisochromatic dot plate concept and a stepped gray scale for contrast discrimination.

sections.heading: Administering and Maintaining Certification
sections.body:
Vision testing only counts if it is done and recorded properly. The examination is administered by a qualified person or facility designated in the written practice - often an eye-care professional, an occupational-health provider, or trained in-house personnel using approved charts under controlled lighting and distance. The result becomes part of the examiner's certification record and must be **traceable**: who was tested, by whom, with which chart, at what distance, the result, and the date.

Three rules a Level II should carry:

- **Corrected vision is acceptable.** Glasses or contacts that bring the examiner to standard are fine; the test is performed with the correction normally worn, and that correction must then be used on the job.
- **Requalification is periodic.** Near acuity is re-tested on the defined interval - commonly at least annually - and color perception is re-evaluated per the practice. A lapsed vision exam can invalidate certification.
- **Failure means restriction, not necessarily the end.** An examiner who fails may retest after correction, or be limited to examinations that do not require the trait they lack.

The thread through all of it: the employer's written practice, aligned to the governing qualification standard, dictates the charts, distances, passing criteria, intervals, and records. Read it, follow it, and keep your vision documentation current.

checkQuestions.question: Why do VT vision examinations emphasize near-vision acuity over distance acuity?
options: Distance vision is impossible to measure
Almost all VT is close-range reading of fine detail
Near vision never changes with age
checkQuestions.explanation: VT is dominated by close-range examination of fine detail and reports, so the standards concentrate on near-vision acuity and require it frequently.

checkQuestions.question: A common VT near-vision requirement is the ability to read:
options: Jaeger 2 (J-2) or smaller at the card's stated distance
Any line on a Snellen chart at twenty feet
checkQuestions.explanation: A typical requirement is reading Jaeger J-2 (or equivalent) at the distance printed on the card, commonly around twelve inches, in at least one eye, corrected or uncorrected.

checkQuestions.question: On a Snellen distance chart, a result of 20/40 means the examinee:
options: Reads at 20 feet what a normal eye reads at 40 feet - worse than standard
Read the chart from 40 feet away
checkQuestions.explanation: In 20/40 the top number is the test distance and the bottom is the distance a normal eye reads that line; 20/40 is worse than the 20/20 standard.

checkQuestions.question: Pseudoisochromatic plate tests are used in VT qualification to:
checkQuestions.explanation: Pseudoisochromatic (colored-dot) plates reveal color-vision deficiency, which is screened because many examinations depend on distinguishing colors and shades.

checkQuestions.question: Regarding VT vision certification, which statement is correct?
options: Once passed, vision never needs re-testing
Corrected vision is acceptable and near acuity is re-tested periodically, commonly at least annually
checkQuestions.explanation: Corrected vision meeting the standard is acceptable, and near-vision acuity is re-examined on a defined interval (commonly at least annually) per the written practice.

references: SNT-TC-1A / NAS410 (vision acuity and color examination requirements, general practice)
Jaeger / Snellen near- and distance-acuity chart conventions (general optometric practice)
